title: Quantum Occam Learning: Sample-Supported Expressibility for Circuit-Based Quantum Learning
authors: Jeongho Bang, Kyoungho Cho, Jeongwoo Jae
abstract: A central principle in quantum machine learning is that an ansatz should be expressive enough to represent the quantum data of interest. Yet, the expressibility is statistically meaningful only insofar as it can be learned from finitely many copies of an unknown quantum state. In this work, we develop an information-theoretic Occam theory for quantum data generated by finite-size quantum circuits. For the class $S_{n,G}$ of $n$-qubit pure states preparable with at most $G$ two-qubit gates, a metric-entropy argument gives the realizable sample law $\widetildeΘ(G/ε^2)$ in the circuit-limited regime. For an arbitrary source $\hatρ$, we introduce the best $G$-gate approximation error $d_G(\hatρ)$ and the approximate circuit complexity $C_η(\hatρ)$. We prove an agnostic quantum Occam theorem: with $M$ copies, one can learn up to the best $G$-gate approximation error plus a statistical penalty $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{G/M})$. We then remove the need to know $G$ in advance through an adaptive model-selection theorem whose oracle inequality selects the circuit complexity justified by the data. Matching lower bounds yield a sample-supported expressibility law: at trace-distance accuracy $ε$, $M$ samples can support only $G_{\rm supported} \simeq Mε^2$ gates, up to logarithmic factors and tomography saturation at $2^n$. Thus, the circuit complexity becomes an adaptive statistical resource rather than a static promise. Our framework turns bounded circuit complexity into a model-selection principle for quantum machine learning.
summary: The paper introduces an Occam theory for quantum learning to quantify how much model expressibility (circuit depth $G$) can be reliably inferred from limited quantum samples ($M$). It derives a fundamental law stating that the supported complexity scales as $M\epsilon^2$, thereby transforming circuit design from a static assumption into a data-dependent statistical resource.
main_problem: The work develops an information-theoretic Occam theory for quantum machine learning to reconcile the expressive power of quantum circuits with the statistical limitations imposed by finite sample data.
main_result: It establishes a 'sample-supported expressibility law' showing that the number of gates ($G$) statistically supported by $M$ samples at accuracy $\epsilon$ scales as $G_{	ext{supported}} \simeq M\epsilon^2$, making circuit complexity an adaptive statistical resource.
method: The analysis uses metric entropy arguments and structural risk minimization analogues to derive bounds on the achievable error, balancing approximation bias ($d_G(\hat{ho})$) against a sample-dependent statistical penalty term.
model_or_system: The system involves estimating an unknown quantum state ($\hat{ho}$) using variational quantum circuits (ansatze) parameterized by a gate count $G$. The learning is performed in the trace distance metric on $n$-qubit systems.
key_observables: Trace-distance error ($\epsilon$), Approximation Error ($d_G(\hat{ho})$), and Sample Size ($M$).
important_parameters: The relationship $G_{	ext{supported}} \simeq M\epsilon^2$ defines the critical boundary between statistically supported and unsupported model complexity.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on bounding the metric entropy of the circuit class using nets to approximate continuous parameter spaces, assuming the learning is performed in an agnostic setting (unknown $\hat{ho}$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the tradeoff between approximation error and statistical penalty, culminating in a clear visualization of the supported expressibility boundary $G_{	ext{supported}} = \min\{2^n, M\epsilon^2\}$.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing basic bounds for realizable learning to developing agnostic theorems that incorporate model selection penalties. The final structure culminates in deriving the sample-supported law by minimizing a penalized objective function over circuit complexity.
why_interesting: This provides a rigorous, information-theoretic framework for determining when quantum machine learning models are statistically justified by data, which is crucial for designing practical variational quantum algorithms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illustrative approximation-estimation tradeoff for M = 5.0 × 104. The dashed curves are stylized approximation errors dG, the solid curves add the statistical radius p

FIG. 2. Sample-supported expressibility boundary for n = 20. The curves show Gsupported = min{2n, Mε2} for several target accuracies, ignoring logarithmic factors. The horizontal dotted line is the tomography saturation 220.
